title: Experimental high-dimensional multi-qubit Bell non-locality on a superconducting quantum processor
authors: Yousef Mafi, Ali G. Moghaddam, Teemu Ojanen
abstract: Combining recent advances in superconducting quantum hardware, we explore quantum correlations in a previously inaccessible regime by observing \emph{simultaneously} high-dimensional and many-body Bell non-locality. We report a high-confidence Bell violation in the correlations between two $d=64$-dimensional systems encoded in twelve qubits. For system sizes up to $d=32$, the strength of the observed nonlocal correlations exceeds the quantum upper bound for $d=2$ systems, providing direct evidence of high-dimensional nonlocality. Furthermore, we demonstrate that the observed violation is genuinely collective: all qubits contribute to the nonlocal correlations, while most pairwise correlations across the bipartition remain Bell-local. Our work illustrates how present-day quantum processors enable the exploration of fundamental predictions of quantum mechanics in previously inaccessible regimes and, in turn, how fundamental quantum effects can be used to benchmark their performance.
summary: This paper reports the experimental observation of high-dimensional, many-body Bell non-locality on a superconducting quantum processor. By using up to 12 qubits to create 64-dimensional systems, the researchers showed that the observed non-locality is a collective phenomenon that cannot be explained by simple pairwise entanglement. They utilized advanced techniques like Dynamic QFT and error mitigation to overcome noise. This work establishes a new way to certify the complex, multi-qubit correlations essential for large-scale quantum computing.
main_problem: Exploring and verifying high-dimensional, many-body Bell non-locality in a regime where non-locality is collective and hidden from pairwise correlations.
main_result: Demonstrated a high-confidence Bell violation for two 64-dimensional systems using 12 qubits, proving that the non-locality is genuinely collective and not reducible to pairwise entanglement.
method: Used a superconducting quantum processor to implement both unitary and Dynamic Quantum Fourier Transform (DQFT) circuits, combined with dynamical decoupling and measurement error mitigation.
model_or_system: A 156-qubit IBM superconducting quantum processor (ibm aachen) using fixed-frequency transmon qubits in a heavy-hex lattice architecture.
key_observables: CGLMP inequality parameter (I_Z,d^ZG), p-values for LHV models, and CHSH inequality for pairwise analysis.
important_parameters: System dimensions up to d=64, 12-qubit partitions, and gate fidelities (single-qubit >99.9%, two-qubit >99%).
main_assumptions: The communication loophole is not addressed as the experiment is performed on a single chip; assumes the validity of the null hypothesis for p-value calculation.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows the Bell test schematic, circuit implementations (QFT vs DQFT), and p-values. Fig 2 illustrates single-qubit perturbation analysis. Fig 3 shows pairwise correlation analysis. Fig 4 compares resource requirements (depth/gate counts). Fig 5 shows p-value scaling with measurement trials.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of high-dimensional many-body non-locality, describes the experimental platform and circuit techniques (DQFT, error mitigation), presents the results of Bell violations and collective nature proofs, and concludes with a discussion on scalability and hardware benchmarking.
why_interesting: It demonstrates a new regime of 'hidden' non-locality that emerges only through collective many-body correlations, providing a powerful new method for benchmarking the performance of large-scale quantum processors.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. High-dimensional non-locality: schematics and results. (a) Schematic representation of a high-dimensional Bell test with two parties. (b) Quantum circuit for the multi-qubit Bell test (c) Circuit implementation of a QFT and a DQFT (d) Results for different QFT-based circuits. (e) Results for the DQFT-based circuits. (e) p-values characterizing the likelihood of a LHV description for the experimental results.

FIG. 2. Unraveling the collective nature of nonlocal- ity via single-qubit perturbations (a) Circuit to probe the participation of an individual qubit to the observed non- locality. Each qubit is individually subjected to a rotation. (b) Evolution of the CGLMP parameter as a function of single-qubit rotations. The blue quadruplet illustrates the ef- fect of rotations of each qubit in a subsystem with four qubits, the red quadruplet illustrates the quantum simulation results.

FIG. 3. High-dimensional non-locality against pair- wise non-locality (a) Schematic of the circuit to evaluate pairwise marginal probabilities obtained from the full proba- bility. While the circuit is identical to that in Fig. 1 (a), the measurements results are separately obtained for each pair i, j. (b) The pairwise Bell function Imax corresponding to CHSH tests. We see only one pair shows siginificant violation while the rest are either slightly violating or Bell local.

FIG. 4. Resource comparison of multi-qubit Bell test on a quantum processor (a)-(b) Scaling of single- and two- qubit gate counts, respectively, with system size. (c)-(d) Cor- responding circuit depths for each implementation. (e) Exe- cution time, highlighting the overall resource requirements of the protocol.

FIG. 5. Statistical significance of IZG d inequality p-value as a function of the number of attempts (measurements or trials) for different system sizes. (a) Results for QFT-based circuits. (b) Results for DQFT-based circuits.
